straight_beat.name: Terrence 'Terry' Boykin
straight_beat.column:
The Orioles lost again. Not in a way that feels inevitable, not in a way that feels like a foregone conclusion, but in a way that feels like a missed opportunity — a chance to seize control of their own narrative, to carve out a little space in the standings, to remind everyone that this team is still here, still trying, still… something. The Giants didn’t beat them. They didn’t even really beat them. They just didn’t let them beat them.  

The offense, for once, didn’t fold under pressure. Rutschman homered. Ramirez doubled. Those two runs — the kind that make you think, *maybe, just maybe* — were there. But then the bullpen, that fragile thing, that box, jar, margin, collapsed. A walk, a wild pitch, a shaky inning, and suddenly the lead was gone. The Giants didn’t do much, but they didn’t have to. They just had to stay alive long enough for the Orioles to make a mistake.  

This is the thing about rebuilding. You don’t just build a team; you build a culture. You build a way of thinking, a way of playing, a way of surviving when the odds are against you. The Orioles have that now. They’ve got the tools, the young arms, the bats with upside. But they also have the ghosts of the past — the lingering questions about whether this team can finish games, whether it can trust its own process, whether it can stop being the team that lets opportunities slip through its fingers.  

Look at

optimist.name: Miz Dottie Hargrove
optimist.column:
The Giants had their way with us yesterday, sure — 2 to 6, and the standings? Still a game back in the AL East. But here’s the thing, hon: you don’t measure a team by a single game. You measure ‘em by the heartbeat of their young core, the way they step into the ballpark with that same fire the ’66 team had when they lit up the world.  

Henderson, Holliday, Rutschman. Three names you’re gonna tell your grandkids you saw at Camden in the spring of their legend. Let’s start with Henderson. The kid’s got a cannon for an arm, and yesterday, he showed why. That seventh-inning fastball? It was like a fastball from a different era, zipping past hitters with that same precision that made the ’66 team unstoppable. Holliday, now, he’s the kind of player who makes you believe in the future. That double in the third? It wasn’t just a hit — it was a statement. A reminder that this team isn’t just built on past glory but on the promise of what’s coming. And Rutschman? The kid’s got the poise of a veteran and the hunger of a kid who’s just starting to figure out how to play this game. He pitched like a man who’s already seen the top of the mountain and is determined to climb it again.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: “Dottie, they lost the game!” And you’re right. They did. But here’s the thing — every loss is a lesson, and every lesson is a step toward something bigger. The ’66 team didn’t just win games; they rewrote the narrative of what a baseball team could be. This core? They’re doing the same thing. They’re not just chasing wins; they’re building a legacy.  

You can’t measure that in a

pessimist.name: Roy Spanulli
pessimist.column:
The Orioles lost again. That’s not the point. The point is that they’re still losing, and the ghosts of the Angelos era are still in the dugout, whispering in the ears of the players, coaching staff, and front office. You think the 100-win season was a fluke? No. It was a mirage. A temporary reflection in a puddle that vanished the moment the sun rose. Now, we’re back to square one, or worse—back to the same old loop of mediocrity, wrapped in the same tired playbook that’s been handed down like a family heirloom.  

The Giants didn’t just beat the Orioles yesterday. They exposed the cracks in this team’s foundation. A 6-2 loss? That’s not a game; it’s a referendum. The Giants, for all their flaws, play with a purpose. They don’t waver. They don’t let the weight of expectations crush them. The Orioles? They’re like a crab that’s lost its spice—tangy on the surface, but hollow inside. You can taste the decay.  

And let’s not pretend the front office is doing anything new.
